channel.title: NYT Book Reviews (Filtered)
channel.description: Actual book reviews from the New York Times, filtered from the noisy Books RSS feed.

item.title: ‘A Good Person,’ by Kirsten King
item.description: The sloppy, solipsistic narrator of Kirsten King’s novel, “A Good Person,” casts a witchy spell on a guy who dumped her. Hours later, he’s been stabbed to death.
item.author: Cat Marnell

item.title: ‘Son of Nobody,’ by Yann Martel
item.description: Yann Martel’s “Son of Nobody” joins many recent books that reimagine the classics, but offers a Nabokovian twist.
item.author: Daniel Mendelsohn

item.title: ‘The Confessions of Samuel Pepys,’ by Guy de la Bédoyère
item.description: Samuel Pepys’s journals are an invaluable record of British history. A new book reconsiders his infamous sexual exploits.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘The Witch,’ by Marie NDiaye
item.description: Part horror, part fable, the latest novel by Marie NDiaye to be translated into English is an exacting portrait of domestic entrapment and psychological turmoil.
item.author: Emily Eakin

item.title: ‘Transcription,’ by Ben Lerner
item.description: In “Transcription,” Ben Lerner considers a famous father, a loyal protégé and a distant son, bound by devotion and separated by miscommunication.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Keeper,’ by Tana French
item.description: “The Keeper,” the final book in her Cal Hooper trilogy, returns readers to an insular village in rural western Ireland.
item.author: Sarah Lyall

item.title: ‘A Potion, a Powder, a Little Bit of Magic: Or, Like Lightning in an Umbrella Storm,’ by Philip Stead
item.description: Philip Stead’s “A Potion, a Powder, a Little Bit of Magic” gleefully ignores all the storytelling rules.
item.author: Adam Rubin

item.title: ‘The Making and Breaking of the American Constitution,’ by Mark Peterson
item.description: In a new book, the historian Mark Peterson argues that our founding document is rooted in ideals of expansion and conquest ill suited to the nation we’ve become.
item.author: Claire Rydell Arcenas

item.title: ‘The Insatiable Machine,’ by Trevor Jackson
item.description: A new history by Trevor Jackson argues that the economic system that transformed global living standards depends on endless growth impossible to sustain.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘How Flowers Made Our World,’ by David George Haskell
item.description: In “How Flowers Made Our World,” David George Haskell makes a case for their soft power.
item.author: Adam Nicolson

item.title: ‘The Universal Baseball Association,’ by Robert Coover
item.description: Just in time for Opening Day, Robert Coover’s prescient 1968 baseball novel is back in print.
item.author: Adam Dalva

item.title: ‘American Men,’ by Jordan Ritter Conn; ‘Who Needs Friends,’ by Andrew McCarthy
item.description: “American Men,” by Jordan Ritter Conn, and “Who Needs Friends,” by Andrew McCarthy, report from the front lines of the epidemic of male loneliness.
item.author: Mark Oppenheimer

item.title: ‘A Treacherous Secret Agent,’ by Marjorie Garber
item.description: In a new book, the Harvard scholar Marjorie Garber suggests how Americans targeted during the Red Scare used literature to confound their interrogators.
item.author: A.O. Scott

item.title: ‘Open Space,’ by David Ariosto
item.description: “Open Space,” by David Ariosto, suggests there are few limits on human ingenuity that could prevent us from colonizing the cosmos.
item.author: Adam Becker

item.title: ‘Darkology,’ by Rhae Lynn Barnes
item.description: A new book by Rhae Lynn Barnes examines how minstrelsy once occupied the center of the nation’s cultural life.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Almost Life,’ by Kiran Millwood Hargrave
item.description: In Kiran Millwood Hargrave’s novel “Almost Life,” a passionate love affair between two college women gives way to a lifetime of what-ifs.
item.author: Stacey D’Erasmo

item.title: ‘Playmakers: The Jewish Entrepreneurs Who Created the Toy Industry in America,’ by Michael Kimmel
item.description: In “Playmakers,” Michael Kimmel traces, and celebrates, the immigrant roots of the American toy industry. (Batteries not included.)
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Feather Wars,’ by James H. McCommons
item.description: In “The Feather Wars,” James H. McCommons pays tribute to the nation’s first conservationists.
item.author: Joshua Hammer

item.title: ‘Everybody’s Fly,’ by Fab 5 Freddy
item.description: As his new memoir demonstrates, he himself would achieve fame as a visual artist, filmmaker, TV host and formative tastemaker.
item.author: Alan Light

item.title: ‘Paradiso 17,’ by Hannah Lillith Assadi
item.description: “Paradiso 17,” by Hannah Lillith Assadi, considers the toll of displacement through the tale of a Palestinian émigré.
item.author: Zain Khalid

item.title: ‘A Scandal in Königsberg,’ by Christopher Clark
item.description: A new book by the historian Christopher Clark chronicles a nearly 200-year-old scandal with echoes of the present day.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘We’ and ‘The People Can Fly,’ by Joshua Bennett
item.description: Joshua Bennett’s two new collections, “We” and “The People Can Fly,” take different paths to the same destination.
item.author: Holly Bass

item.title: ‘Chain of Ideas,’ by Ibram X. Kendi
item.description: In “Chain of Ideas,” Ibram X. Kendi argues that a modern form of xenophobia has come to dominate conservative movements across the world.
item.author: Sam Adler-Bell

item.title: ‘Salt Lakes,’ by Caroline Tracey
item.description: In a new book, Caroline Tracey explores the mysteries and beauty of salt lakes.
item.author: Robert Sullivan

item.title: ‘Sisters in Yellow,’ by Mieko Kawakami
item.description: Mieko Kawakami’s novel “Sisters in Yellow” follows a group of dreaming and scheming young women through society’s margins.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Stay Alive,’ by Ian Buruma
item.description: In “Stay Alive,” Ian Buruma paints a picture of the city dwellers who survived in Germany under the Nazis.
item.author: Kevin Peraino

item.title: ‘The Secret History of French Cooking,’ by Luke Barr
item.description: A new history by Luke Barr chronicles the innovations, excesses and chauvinism of the French chefs who spawned a revolution in cooking.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Natural Way of Things,’ by Charlotte Wood
item.description: Charlotte Wood’s “The Natural Way of Things” conjures a not-so-implausible world in which girls and young women are thrown into prison for their sexual shames.
item.author: Leah Greenblatt

item.title: ‘Books Good Enough for You: The Storied Life of Ursula Nordstrom, Editor of Extraordinary Children’s Books,’ by Nancy Hudgins
item.description: From 1940 to 1973, Ursula Nordstrom transformed kids’ books into real art and big business. A new middle grade biography attempts to capture her magic.
item.author: Mac Barnett

item.title: ‘Night Night Fawn,’ by Jordy Rosenberg
item.description: Jordy Rosenberg’s second novel, “Night Night Fawn,” approaches a closed-minded matriarch with compassion, even at her child’s expense.
item.author: Melissa Broder

item.title: ‘In the Days of My Youth I Was Told What It Means to Be a Man,’ by Tom Junod
item.description: In an affecting new memoir, Tom Junod, a prizewinning magazine writer, grapples with unsettling discoveries about his larger-than-life dad.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘The Golden Boy,’ by Patricia Finn
item.description: From his perch in Hawaii, the hero of Patricia Finn’s first novel, “The Golden Boy,” revisits his dark past in rural Ontario.
item.author: Beatriz Williams

item.title: ‘Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! A Memoir,’ by Liza Minnelli
item.description: “Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!” is a familiar reminder that growing up in showbiz can lead to awards and adulation, but also to heartache.
item.author: Alexandra Jacobs

item.title: ‘The Complex,’ by Karan Mahajan
item.description: Karan Mahajan’s new novel, “The Complex,” tracks the fortunes of a political family in a rapidly changing India.
item.author: Jonathan Dee

item.title: ‘Down Time,’ by Andrew Martin
item.description: In Andrew Martin’s keenly observed new novel, a group of friends navigate a society reshaped by the pandemic.
item.author: Ben Greenman

item.title: ‘Nonesuch,’ by Francis Spufford
item.description: “Nonesuch,” the new novel by Francis Spufford, conjures a plot laced with magic to change the course of history.
item.author: Louisa Hall

item.title: ‘Whidbey,’ by T Kira Madden
item.description: In “Whidbey,” three women reckon with the aftermath of sexual assault.
item.author: Catherine Chidgey

item.title: ‘Gunk,’ by Saba Sams
item.description: “Gunk,” a novel by Saba Sams, follows a woman through the trials and tenuous jobs of young adulthood.
item.author: Dwight Garner

item.title: ‘Stories,’ by Helen Garner
item.description: A newly released collection of the Australian master’s short fiction shows her sympathy, her virtuosity and her ear.
item.author: Joumana Khatib

item.title: ‘Chosen Land,’ by Matthew Avery Sutton
item.description: In “Chosen Land,” Matthew Avery Sutton argues that, despite the intentions of certain founders, the First Amendment guaranteed that the United States would be a godly country.
item.author: Brenda Wineapple

item.title: ‘Days of Love and Rage,’ by Anand Gopal
item.description: In “Days of Love and Rage,” Anand Gopal creates an indelible portrait of revolution and civil war in Syria.
item.author: Charles Glass

item.title: ‘Lake Effect,’ by Cynthia D’Aprix Sweeney
item.description: Cynthia D’Aprix Sweeney’s new novel, “Lake Effect,” is the latest in a specific contemporary subgenre: “Four Adult Siblings Reconvene to Rehash Their Privileged but Fraught Adolescence.”
item.author: Rebecca Makkai

item.title: ‘Reproductive Wrongs,’ by Sarah Ruden
item.description: In “Reproductive Wrongs,” the classicist Sarah Ruden traces efforts to exert political control over family planning back 2,000 years.
item.author: Jennifer Szalai

item.title: ‘Plastic Inc.,’ by Beth Gardiner
item.description: A new book by the journalist Beth Gardiner argues that oil companies are upping production of the material as a safeguard against falling revenue.
item.author: Zoë Schlanger

item.title: ‘Every Time We Say Goodbye,’ by Ivana Sajko
item.description: Ivana Sajko’s novel “Every Time We Say Goodbye” explores personal and political crises in lengthy, lyrical sentences.
item.author: Ken Kalfus
